metadata.title: Biologically-inspired adaptive learning in the Hopfield-network based self-optimization model

sections.title: Biological learning is different from machine learning in key ways
Self-optimization (SO) model
content: The SO model is a Hopfield network with:
updating of weights based on a Hebbian learning rule

sections.title: Example: learning rate as a function of energy
content: 1. Weight update rule (α: learning rate; s_i, s_j: states of nodes i and j)
w_ij(t + 1) = w_ij(t) + dw_ij(t),    dw_ij = α s_i s_j
2. Learning rate α as a function of energy E:
α = { a / (ΔE + ε_0) if γ ≠ 1; α_0 if γ = 1 }
SO model results for map-coloring problem. From left to right, E=5, E=8, E=12, E=20 (only the two middle results are valid maps).
Implementing mechanisms used byliving systemstoadaptto their environment may helpsolve trade-offsinherent to machine learning.

sections.title: Translating adaptive biological mechanisms into a Hopfield network

figures.description: Diagram of biological mechanisms like Metaplasticity, Homeostasis, and Modularity mapped to network functions

content: Energy distributions at the end of learning for 1000 runs. A slight difference can be observed between γ=1 (learning rate α constant) and γ=0.1 (variable learning rate), but not obviously significant.

sections.title: What next?
content: Test further implementations of variable learning rate: as function of weights, trigonometric function
Applications in continuous learning, creativity, robotics

sections.title: References
content: W. C. Abraham. Metaplasticity: Tuning synapses and networks for plasticity. Nat Rev Neurosci, 9(5):387–387, 2008-05H. Kitano. Biological robustness. Nat Rev Genet, 5(11):826–837, 2004-11R. A. Watson, C. L. Buckley, and R. Mills. Optimization in "self-modeling" complex adaptive systems. Complexity, 16(5):17–26, 2011

sections.title: Acknowledgments
content: I would like to sincerely thank Natalya Weber (OIST) for her work on the implementation example and for producing the figures in this poster.
